input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: 1/10 scale autonomous vehicle (teleoperated and retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology) used as a test platform for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). The platform includes a lower and upper chassis and autonomous elements such as ACC logic, sensors, and control units. It operates in a controlled test environment and demonstrates resource‑constrained ECUs typical of production vehicles, making it ideal for digital twin studies that focus on computation offload and remote management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Three core services are provided: (1) Data Monitoring – a real‑time UI displays vehicle state, projected path, and nearby object tracks, allows saving data and generating reports; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm runs on remote cloud hardware and the desired speed is returned to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and send emergency braking via the UI. Services target operators for monitoring and controlling ACC, and developers for debugging and troubleshooting, supporting low end‑to‑end latency and safety‑critical communication.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The twin enforces 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy between physical and digital components, in line with safety‑critical vehicular standards that require stricter than 100 ms synchronization. This precision is driven by hardware limitations and is achieved by time‑synchronisation protocols used during computation offloading and device coordination.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system adopts a decentralized architecture with three distinct functional entities – User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin – each deployed on separate hardware units. Although only one Digital Twin instance is described, the entity‑based decomposition allows for hierarchical orchestration: the Physical Twin Entity hosts core automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking, the Digital Twin Entity manages data storage and control mechanisms, and the User Entity exposes 3D visualization and control panels.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows ISO 23247 reference architecture: software components are mapped to functional entities (FEs) and instantiated as individual ROS 2 packages containing one or more nodes. The Digital Twin entity encapsulates data management and control mechanisms; the Physical Twin entity incorporates data pre‑processing, actuation, and device communication; and the User entity provides the user interface split into visualization and control panels. Each FE resides on dedicated hardware, enabling localized resource management and global system‑wide coordination through ROS 2 middleware.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensors such as LiDAR, vehicle state (IMU, speed, wheel angle), motor and steering servo actuators generate raw data streams – point clouds, actuator status and vehicle state parameters. The Data Translation Front‑End serializes these streams, normalises message types, and performs unit conversions before forwarding them over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP to the Digital Twin framework. Inside the framework a ROS2 network distributes the data to processing nodes; the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component preprocesses raw LiDAR point clouds, extracting object tracks and projecting them into a 2‑D plane for downstream services. The processed information is then consumed by the Data Monitoring service, which pushes vehicle state, object tracks, and projected driving paths to the 3D Visualization FE in real time. This flow is event‑driven for each incoming sensor frame; however, the document does not specify explicit transmission frequencies, throttling, or detailed data quality assurance mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components inside the Digital Twin is orchestrated via a ROS2 network that utilizes DDS for message passing. External inter‑entity communication – for example, between the vehicle’s physical sensors and the twin framework – is carried over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. The document does not provide detailed specifications for network bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy or security mechanisms such as encryption or authentication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin components – including the Data Translation FE, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE, and Data Monitoring FE – are deployed as ROS2 nodes on the vehicle’s on‑board processing hardware, with inter‑entity communication over local Wi‑Fi. The architecture illustrated in the figures implies an on‑board edge deployment, but the document does not detail cloud or on‑premise hosting options, computational or storage requirements, scalability strategies, or availability and reliability assurance mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with ISO 23247—Digital twin framework for manufacturing, specifically Part 2 which defines an entity‑based reference architecture. The Data Collection and Device Control Entity may be implemented using OPC Unified Architecture to enable interoperability with other manufacturing systems. No additional certification or compliance frameworks are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The Digital Twin system follows the data‑governance model prescribed by 3GPP TS 22.185 for V2X services: the vehicle’s manufacturer retains ownership of all per‑vehicle data, while V2X traffic‑related data (position, speed, alerts) are treated as personal data under GDPR.  The system implements consent management through a privacy agent that issues a one‑time consent token for each V2X session. All transmitted V2X messages are pseudonymised, with the pseudonym rotated every 10 seconds using a cryptographically secure random number generator as defined in TS 22.185.  Data exchanges with roadside infrastructure are protected by mutually authenticated 5G links employing EAP‑TLS and a public‑key infrastructure compliant with ISO/IEC 27001.  Bilateral data‑sharing agreements are recorded, detailing the purpose, retention period and the recipient’s obligations, ensuring compliance with GDPR Article 5 and 6.  Non‑personal data used by the Adaptive Cruise Control (e.g., sensor readings for control logic) are stored locally in the vehicle network and exempt from GDPR processing, while cockpit configuration data accessed through the Digital Twin interface are protected by role‑based access control enforced by an attribute‑based policy engine referenced in ISO 26262‑1 for safety‑critical data handling.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The system’s security architecture incorporates the layered cyber‑security model of 3GPP TS 22.185: all V2X traffic is encrypted with 5G‑specific confidentiality mechanisms (AES‑128‑GCM) and authenticated via mutual TLS with short‑lived certificates.  An intrusion‑detection module monitors V2X traffic for anomalous patterns and triggers a rapid fail‑safe state when thresholds are exceeded.  The Digital Twin cockpit employs a role‑based access control policy backed by short‑lived digital certificates, compliant with ISO 26262‑1 safety requirements for safety‑critical functions.  Functional safety is addressed according to the safety concept outlined in ISO 26262‑1: hazard analysis, safety goals, and the implementation of a safety‑critical Adaptive Cruise Control logic that includes an automatic emergency brake routine activated when a fault in speed or braking torque sensors is detected.  Redundancy and diagnostic checks are performed at the system level to meet the SIL requirements specified for safety‑critical components, ensuring that the V2X‑based decision logic remains reliable under fault conditions.  Software and security updates are delivered via OTA with cryptographically signed packages and an audit trail of hash values to guarantee integrity as required by ISO 26262‑2.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
